title: Unit 5 — The AI Analyst
text:
Everything in this primer has been preparing you for a working life where you’ll rarely run the code yourself. You’ll ask an AI tool to do it — and the tool will be fast, fluent, confident, and exactly as good as the questions you ask it. It will make reasonable-sounding decisions without telling you. Some of them will be wrong in ways that are invisible in the output and obvious in the code.
This unit is a simulation of that. You’re an analyst with a 3 PM deadline, an AI assistant named Ada, and the ten-state dataset you know from Units 1 and 3. Ada is genuinely capable — it will also, like every analyst (and every AI), make judgment calls and mistakes. Every trap in this story is one you’ve already met in Units 1–4. Your job is to catch them before they reach the Secretary.






TipHow to play



Read each exchange, then choose what to say. There are seven endings — some triumphant, several instructively embarrassing. Use Rewind to back up one choice and try another road, or Start over to replay from the top. Your place is saved if you leave the page. There’s no grade; the endings are the feedback — finding the bad ones on purpose is a perfectly good way to learn.


One note: Ada is scripted — this is a story, not a live AI. (The 💬 helper in the corner is the live one, and yes, you can ask it for hints here too. It knows the rules.)

title: Unit 5 — The AI Analyst
text:
Everything in this primer has been preparing you for a working life where you’ll rarely run the code yourself. You’ll ask an AI tool to do it — and the tool will be fast, fluent, confident, and exactly as good as the questions you ask it. It will make reasonable-sounding decisions without telling you. Some of them will be wrong in ways that are invisible in the output and obvious in the code.
This unit is a simulation of that. You’re an analyst with a 3 PM deadline, an AI assistant named Ada, and the ten-state dataset you know from Units 1 and 3. Ada is genuinely capable — it will also, like every analyst (and every AI), make judgment calls and mistakes. Every trap in this story is one you’ve already met in Units 1–4. Your job is to catch them before they reach the Secretary.






TipHow to play



Read each exchange, then choose what to say. There are seven endings — some triumphant, several instructively embarrassing. Use Rewind to back up one choice and try another road, or Start over to replay from the top. Your place is saved if you leave the page. There’s no grade; the endings are the feedback — finding the bad ones on purpose is a perfectly good way to learn.


One note: Ada is scripted — this is a story, not a live AI. (The 💬 helper in the corner is the live one, and yes, you can ask it for hints here too. It knows the rules.)

title: Unit 5 — The AI Analyst
section: After you’ve found a few endings
text:
After you’ve found a few endings
Three questions worth a minute of reflection:

Which mistakes would you have caught a month ago, before starting this primer?
Every bad ending shared a root cause: a decision the AI made silently. What questions force those decisions into the open? (“How is this weighted?” “Show me the coded variable.” “How many rows after the join?”)
The best ending didn’t come from distrusting the AI — Ada did all the work, fast. It came from supervising it like a colleague. What’s the difference?

That instinct — delegate the typing, never the judgment — is the single most useful thing you can carry into the fall, and into every meeting after that.

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
text:
Everything you read in Units 1 and 2 was correct code. That ends now.
Each exercise below shows you a short analysis and the claim an analyst made from it. The code runs without errors and the output looks plausible — but in every case something is wrong: a mistaken assumption, a silent data problem, or a number that doesn’t mean what the analyst says it means. R will not warn you. The only defense is reading carefully and thinking about what the numbers should look like.
For each exercise:

Describe what the code actually did (the Unit 2 skill).
Identify what’s wrong.
Explain why the output is misleading — ideally using what you know about the policy world, not just the code.

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
text:
Everything you read in Units 1 and 2 was correct code. That ends now.
Each exercise below shows you a short analysis and the claim an analyst made from it. The code runs without errors and the output looks plausible — but in every case something is wrong: a mistaken assumption, a silent data problem, or a number that doesn’t mean what the analyst says it means. R will not warn you. The only defense is reading carefully and thinking about what the numbers should look like.
For each exercise:

Describe what the code actually did (the Unit 2 skill).
Identify what’s wrong.
Explain why the output is misleading — ideally using what you know about the policy world, not just the code.

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
text:
The dataset
We’re back to the ten-state health insurance data from Unit 1 — same columns, same values. It loads automatically; run the chunk to remind yourself what’s in it.

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
text:
Exercise 1 — “The average state”
An analyst wants a single headline number for the briefing and writes:









“Across our ten states, the uninsured rate is 9.0%.”

The arithmetic is correct — the ten rates really do average to 0.09. Now think about what that number represents.

What’s the most important problem with calling this “the uninsured rate across our ten states”?

mean() should have been median() — switching to the median changes the number, but it doesn’t fix what’s conceptually wrong with averaging this column.
Every state counts equally, so Nevada (3 million people) moves the average exactly as much as California (39 million) — right: this is the average of state rates, not the rate for the population of these states. The big high-uninsurance states get diluted.
The rates should have been summed, not averaged — summing rates produces a meaningless number (0.90 = “90%”?); the issue is how the average treats states, not sum vs. mean.


The population-weighted rate — total uninsured people divided by total population — comes out to about 9.7%, noticeably higher than 9.0%, because the biggest states (Texas, Florida, California) have above-average uninsurance. Whenever you see mean() applied to a column that is itself a rate, ask: should every row really count equally?


Try fixing it yourself — edit the chunk below so each state contributes in proportion to its population. (One approach: compute uninsured people with mutate(), then divide totals, like Unit 2’s Exercise 4.)

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
text:
Exercise 2 — The unit trap
The team decides percentages read better than proportions, so an analyst rescales the column and then pulls out the “high-uninsurance” states for a map:









“Ten of our ten states have a high-uninsurance problem.”


Run the chunk. Why did all ten states survive the filter?

The threshold wasn’t rescaled along with the column: after * 100, the values run from 3 to 18, and every number in that range beats 0.10 — right: the filter compares percent-scale values against a proportion-scale threshold.
filter() ran before mutate(), so the threshold applied to the old column — pipelines run top to bottom; mutate() had already created uninsured_pct when filter() used it.
All ten states genuinely have high uninsurance — Massachusetts is at 3%, among the lowest in the nation; an analysis that flags it as high-uninsurance should set off alarm bells.



Write the note you’d send this analyst: what did the code actually do, why is the claim wrong, and what one-character-ish fix would repair it?

The code created a percent version of the uninsured rate (3–18 instead of 0.03–0.18) and then kept states where that percent exceeds 0.10. Since every percent value is far above 0.10, the filter kept all ten states — it did no filtering at all. The claim is wrong because the cutoff was meant on the old scale: “above 0.10” meant “above 10%,” but after multiplying by 100 the matching threshold is 10, not 0.10. The fix is filter(uninsured_pct > 10) (or filter on the original uninsured_rate > 0.10 before rescaling). The giveaway in the output is substantive: a “high uninsurance” list that includes Massachusetts, which famously has near-universal coverage, can’t be right.


A strong answer must (1) describe the rescaling and the filter, (2) pinpoint the unit mismatch — the threshold 0.10 is on the proportion scale while the column is now on the percent scale, so every state passes and the filter is vacuous, and (3) give the fix (compare against 10, or filter before rescaling). Bonus credit (not required for “strong”) for the domain sanity check that Massachusetts appearing on a high-uninsurance list is implausible. “partial” if the student senses the threshold is wrong but can’t articulate the scale mismatch or offers no fix. “needs_work” if they think the code errored, that mutate dropped rows, or that all ten states genuinely have high uninsurance.

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
section: Exercise 3 — The join that counted people twice
text:
Exercise 3 — The join that counted people twice
There’s a second table on this page, programs, listing state outreach programs for insurance enrollment — some states run several, some none:








An analyst joins it to the state data to size up the reach of outreach efforts:









“Outreach programs operate in states that are home to a combined 324 million people.”

That would be nearly the entire population of the United States — from ten states. Run the chunk and look at the rows count before reading on.

Describe what happened in the join, explain how the population total got to 324 million, and state what the correct combined population of these ten states is (roughly).

left_join() matches each state to its outreach programs, and when a state has several programs its row gets duplicated — once per program. California and New York each appear three times, Massachusetts and Texas twice, so the joined table has 16 rows instead of 10. Summing population over those 16 rows counts California’s 39 million three times, New York’s 20 million three times, and so on, inflating the total to about 324 million. The real combined population of the ten states is about 169 million. The lesson: after any join, check the row count before summing anything — duplicated rows turn sums into double-counts. (One more wrinkle: Pennsylvania and Nevada have no programs at all, yet they’re still in the total, so even “deduplicated” this number wouldn’t measure where programs operate.)


A strong answer must (1) explain that the join duplicates a state’s row once per matching program, growing 10 rows to 16, and (2) connect that to the inflated sum — populations of multi-program states are counted multiple times — and give roughly the right true total (anything near 169–170 million, or simply “about half the claimed figure,” is fine). The Pennsylvania/Nevada point (no programs but still counted, since left_join keeps unmatched rows) is bonus, not required. “partial” if the student sees the total is too big and blames the join but can’t explain the duplication mechanism. “needs_work” if they think sum() is the wrong function, that the population column is wrong, or that left_join dropped states.

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
section: Exercise 4 — The regression that proved the opposite
text:
Exercise 4 — The regression that proved the opposite
An analyst merges in a Medicaid expansion indicator and runs the regression the team asked for. Read the code and the data it creates carefully — the comment matters.









“States that expanded Medicaid have uninsured rates about 8 percentage points higher than states that didn’t (p < 0.01). Expansion does not appear to reduce uninsurance.”

Before you write — a gut check:

Based on what you know about Medicaid expansion (or Unit 1’s medicaid table), which states actually expanded by 2022?

Massachusetts, New York, California, and most of the others — Florida, Georgia, and Texas are the famous holdouts — right: which is exactly the opposite of what this expansion table says.
Florida, Georgia, and Texas only — these three are the best-known non-expansion states; coding them as the only expanders should look backwards immediately.
None of these ten states — most states (about 40 by 2022) expanded; an all-zero coding for MA, NY, and CA can’t be right.



Write your memo: what does the regression actually estimate, what went wrong upstream of it, and what would the (corrected) result most likely show? Why is the analyst’s policy conclusion not just wrong but backwards?

The regression compares average uninsured rates between states coded expanded = 1 and expanded = 0; the coefficient (~+0.08) says the “expanded” group runs about 8 points higher. But the indicator is coded backwards: the table assigns 1 to Florida, Georgia, and Texas — the well-known non-expansion states — and 0 to genuine expanders like Massachusetts, New York, and California. The comment says one thing; the data says the opposite. So the regression is really measuring that non-expansion states have higher uninsured rates. With the coding fixed, the same coefficient flips sign: expansion states are about 8 points lower. The analyst’s conclusion is backwards because the only thing wrong was upstream of the model — the regression machinery worked perfectly on miscoded data. (Even corrected, this is a 10-state cross-section, not proof that expansion caused the difference.)


A strong answer must (1) describe what the regression estimates (difference in mean uninsured rate between the two coded groups), (2) identify the upstream error — the 0/1 coding contradicts its own comment and reality, with FL/GA/TX (non-expanders) coded as 1 — caught by domain knowledge or by cross-checking Unit 1’s medicaid table, and (3) state that correcting the coding flips the sign: expansion states have roughly 8 points lower uninsurance, so the policy conclusion is inverted. A causality caveat (cross-sectional, 10 states, expansion not random) is bonus, not required. “partial” if the student senses the result contradicts reality but doesn’t locate the miscoding, or finds the miscoding but can’t say what the corrected result implies. “needs_work” if they accept the analyst’s conclusion or blame lm() itself.

title: Unit 3 — Spot the Problem
text:
The habit to take away
Notice what caught each error: not R skills, but expectations about the world. A 9% average that hides Texas, a “high-uninsurance” list containing Massachusetts, a ten-state population the size of the whole country, an expansion coefficient with the wrong sign. The code ran fine every time. Unit 4 hands you a full script and asks you to bring both skills at once — narrate it, and question it.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
By the time you arrive on campus, you’ll have seen the eight tidyverse “verbs” that make up almost every R script we’ll read together this fall. Your goal isn’t to memorize syntax. It’s to learn to recognize each verb on sight and know what it does to a dataset.
Most code on this page is runnable right in your browser — you can click Run Code under any chunk to see the output, and you can edit the code and re-run it. There’s no R installation, no setup. (The first chunk you run may take 10–20 seconds to load R; after that everything is instant.)






TipHow to use this page



For each verb: read the description, look at the code, and predict what the output will look like before you click Run. Then run it and see if you were right. If not, change the code and try again — that’s the fastest way to build intuition.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
By the time you arrive on campus, you’ll have seen the eight tidyverse “verbs” that make up almost every R script we’ll read together this fall. Your goal isn’t to memorize syntax. It’s to learn to recognize each verb on sight and know what it does to a dataset.
Most code on this page is runnable right in your browser — you can click Run Code under any chunk to see the output, and you can edit the code and re-run it. There’s no R installation, no setup. (The first chunk you run may take 10–20 seconds to load R; after that everything is instant.)






TipHow to use this page



For each verb: read the description, look at the code, and predict what the output will look like before you click Run. Then run it and see if you were right. If not, change the code and try again — that’s the fastest way to build intuition.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
The dataset
Every example on this page uses the same small dataset: 2022 state-level health and income data for ten US states. Think of it as a stripped-down version of what you’d pull from CDC BRFSS combined with Census ACS estimates. The data loads automatically in the background — click Run Code below to see all 10 rows.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
1. The pipe: |>
The pipe is not a verb — it’s the connector between verbs. Read |> as the words “and then.” It takes whatever is on its left and feeds it as the first input to the function on its right.














In plain English: Take the states data, and then keep only the rows where region is South.



You will see long pipelines that read like sentences: take the data, and then filter, and then group, and then summarize. The pipe is what stitches them together.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
2. filter() — keep certain rows
filter() reduces a dataset by keeping only rows that meet a condition. The number of columns doesn’t change. The number of rows usually shrinks.














In plain English: Keep only the states where the uninsured rate is greater than 10%.









TipTry it



Change > 0.10 to < 0.10. Which states show up now? What about >= 0.13? The condition inside filter() is the most important thing to read carefully — > vs >=, == vs !=, and & vs | all change which rows survive.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
3. select() — keep certain columns
select() is the column-wise sibling of filter(). It keeps the columns you name and drops the rest. The number of rows doesn’t change.














In plain English: From the states data, keep only the state and uninsured_rate columns.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
section: 4. mutate() — add or change a column
text:
4. mutate() — add or change a column
mutate() creates a new column or overwrites an existing one. The number of rows stays the same; the number of columns usually grows.














In plain English: Create a new column called uninsured_count equal to the uninsured rate multiplied by the population, then show just the four columns we care about.









TipWhat to look for



mutate() is where a lot of analytic judgment lives. Whenever you see a new column being created, ask: is this the right formula? and what units is the result in? The example above turned a rate into a count by multiplying by population — a small change in arithmetic that completely changes what the column means.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
section: 5. group_by() — flag rows as belonging to groups
text:
5. group_by() — flag rows as belonging to groups
group_by() is the quietest verb: by itself it changes nothing you can see in the data. It just attaches a label saying “treat these rows as groups.” Look closely at the output below — the rows and columns are identical, but there’s a new line near the top: Groups: region [4].














In plain English: Take the states data, and then mark the rows as grouped by region — so whatever comes next happens within each region separately.



group_by() almost never appears alone. It’s the setup for the next verb.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
section: 6. summarize() — collapse many rows into a few
text:
6. summarize() — collapse many rows into a few
summarize() boils a dataset down: many rows in, one row out — or one row per group if the data was grouped first. This group_by() |> summarize() pair is the single most common pattern in policy data analysis.














In plain English: Group the states by region, and then collapse each region to a single row showing its average uninsured rate and how many states it contains.









TipWhat to look for



After summarize(), the original rows are gone — ten states became four regions. When you read a summarize(), always ask: what does one row of the output represent now? The answer changed, and every number in the row is a summary, not a raw value.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
7. arrange() — sort rows
arrange() reorders rows. Nothing is added, nothing is dropped — the data just gets sorted. Wrap a column in desc() to sort largest-first.














In plain English: Sort the states from highest to lowest uninsured rate, and then show just the state name and the rate.



You’ll usually see arrange() at the end of a pipeline, as a presentation step: it answers “which ones are highest?”

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
8. left_join() — combine two tables
Real analyses almost always pull from more than one source. left_join() matches rows from a second table onto the first, using a shared ID column (by = ...). “Left” means: keep every row of the first (left) table, whether or not it finds a match.
There’s a second small table pre-loaded on this page, medicaid, which records whether each state expanded Medicaid:






















In plain English: Take the states data, and then attach each state’s Medicaid expansion status from the medicaid table, matching on the state name.









TipWhat to look for



A join is where datasets quietly grow or shrink. Here every state matched exactly one row, so we still have 10 rows. When you read a join, ask: could a row on the left match zero rows (producing an NA) or several rows (duplicating data)? Unit 3 will show you what happens when an analyst forgets to ask.

title: Unit 1 — The Building Blocks
text:
Check your understanding
Answer the questions below right on this page — you’ll get instant feedback. They aren’t graded; they’re here so you know whether the verbs have stuck before moving on.

1. How should you read the pipe, |>?

“Is equal to” — that’s ==, which appears inside filter() conditions.
“And then” — the pipe takes the result on its left and feeds it into the function on its right.
“Or else” — there’s no fallback behavior here; the pipe always passes its left side along.
“Assign to” — assignment in R is <-, which you’ll see at the start of scripts, not between verbs.



2. Which verb keeps only certain rows of a dataset?

select() — select() is the column-wise sibling: it keeps certain columns.
filter() — right: the condition inside filter() decides which rows survive.
mutate() — mutate() adds or changes a column; it never drops rows.



3. What does this pipeline return?
states |>
  filter(region == "South") |>
  select(state, median_income)

All 10 states, but only the state and median_income columns — the filter() step drops every row that isn’t in the South before select() runs.
Only the southern states, with just the state and median_income columns — filter() shrinks the rows, then select() shrinks the columns.
Only the southern states, with all five columns — select() drops the columns it doesn’t name, so only two remain.


Reading order matters: each verb hands its result to the next one. First the rows shrink (filter), then the columns shrink (select). Scroll up and run the chunks if you want to see each step on its own.



4. After running states |> mutate(uninsured_pct = uninsured_rate * 100), which statement is true?

The dataset has fewer rows — mutate() never changes the number of rows; that’s filter()’s job.
The uninsured_rate column is replaced — a new name on the left of = creates a new column; uninsured_rate is only overwritten if you reuse its exact name.
The dataset has the same rows plus one new column — mutate() adds uninsured_pct and leaves everything else alone.



5. How many rows does this return?
states |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarize(avg_income = mean(median_income))

10 — one per state — the states are gone after summarize(); each region became a single row.
4 — one per region — right: summarize() collapses each group down to one row.
1 — a single overall average — that’s what you’d get without the group_by() line.



6. What does states |> left_join(medicaid, by = "state") do to the number of rows?

Nothing — all 10 states stay, each gaining the expanded_medicaid column — right: a left join keeps every row of the left table, and here each state matches exactly one row.
Keeps only states that appear in both tables — that’s an inner join; left_join() keeps unmatched rows and fills in NA.
Doubles the rows to 20 — rows only multiply when one state matches several rows in the second table; here each matches one.



Explain it in plain English
This is the core skill of the course. Read the pipeline below, then describe what it does in your own words — what data goes in, what each step does, and what the result shows. Write your answer before running the chunk.









In plain English, what does this pipeline do?

It starts with the state-level dataset, keeps only the states with more than 10 million residents, then creates a new column estimating the number of uninsured people in each state by multiplying the uninsured rate by the population. Finally it shows just two columns: the state name and that estimated uninsured count.


Award “strong” only if the answer covers all three steps: (1) filtering to large states (population above 10 million — “big states” is acceptable), (2) creating a new column that converts the uninsured rate into an estimated count of uninsured people by multiplying by population, and (3) the output showing only state and the new count column. The rate-to-count conversion in step 2 is the most important idea — an answer that says the pipeline “adds a column” without conveying that it turns a percentage into a number of people is at best “partial”. Misreading filter (e.g. keeping small states) or claiming rows/columns change in ways they don’t is “needs_work”.

title: Reading R Code
text:
This fall in API-201, you’ll constantly encounter data analysis written in R — in problem sets, in lecture, and in the memos and papers you’ll read and write. Long before you ever write R yourself, you’ll need to do something more basic and more important: look at code someone else wrote and say, in plain English, what it did. What data went in, what happened to it, what the output shows, and what judgment calls the analyst made along the way.
That one skill is what this primer teaches. You will not write code from scratch here, and you don’t need any programming background — just curiosity and roughly ten hours between now and your arrival on campus, in whatever chunks fit your summer.

title: Reading R Code
text:
How this site works
Everything runs right in your browser — there is nothing to install, no account to create, and nothing you can break. The code on these pages is live: click Run Code under any chunk to execute it, edit it, and run it again (that’s encouraged — every chunk has a Start Over button).






NoteGive each page a moment to wake up



The first time you run code on a page, your browser spends about 20–30 seconds loading R behind the scenes (Units 2 and 4 also load a plotting library). That’s normal — after the first run, everything is instant. A laptop works much better than a phone for this.


Along the way you’ll meet three kinds of questions:

Multiple choice — answered right on the page, with instant feedback and a chance to retry.
“Explain it in plain English” — short written answers, the core skill of the course. Click Get feedback for an AI reading of your answer against the instructor’s rubric (it gives a verdict and a pointer, never the answer itself), or Show model answer to compare for yourself.
Predict-then-run — the habit that makes all of this stick: read the code, commit to a guess about the output, then run it.

None of it is graded or tracked. Your answers live only in your own browser, and redoing a page is a feature, not a confession.

title: Reading R Code
text:
The five units
Work through them in order — each one builds on the last.

Unit 1 — The Building Blocks · ~2 hours
The eight tidyverse “verbs” that make up almost every R script you’ll see this fall: the pipe |> plus filter, select, mutate, group_by, summarize, arrange, and left_join. One verb at a time, each with live examples on a small state-level health insurance dataset, ending with a quick quiz to check that the verbs stuck.


Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline · ~3 hours
The verbs start chaining together. Six pipelines on school-district data, growing from three lines to a scatterplot and a full regression. For each one you’ll predict the output, describe it in plain English, and then run it to check yourself. Hints early on; training wheels off by the end.


Unit 3 — Spot the Problem · ~3 hours
Everything in Units 1 and 2 was correct code. Here, nothing is. Four analyses that run without errors and produce plausible-looking output — each hiding a mistake that makes the analyst’s claim wrong, sometimes backwards. Your job: describe what the code did, find the flaw, and explain why the output misleads. The errors are catchable with careful reading and common sense about the policy world — exactly the skill that makes you valuable in a room.


Unit 4 — Full Narration · ~2 hours
The capstone. One complete 35-line analysis script — cleaning, transforming, summarizing, plotting, regression — of the kind a junior analyst might email around. You’ll narrate the whole thing for a colleague who hasn’t seen the code, and flag the one decision you’d push back on before the results get circulated.


Unit 5 — The AI Analyst · ~45 minutes
A choose-your-own-adventure finale. You have a 3 PM deadline, an AI assistant that works fast and decides things silently, and one slide to get right. Seven endings — most of them instructively embarrassing — each turning on whether you catch a mistake you learned to spot in Units 1–4. Replaying to find the other endings is the point.

title: Reading R Code
text:
When you get stuck
You’ll notice a 💬 button in the corner of every page. That’s the primer’s helper — an AI assistant that knows these pages, the datasets, and the exercises. There’s also a small “Help me understand this” link under every code box that sends that snippet straight to it.
Use it — that’s what it’s for — but a word of advice first: being stuck is where the learning happens. Reread the code, run it, change something and run it again, take a real guess. Reach for the helper when you’re genuinely stuck, not at the first flicker of confusion. It’s built to match that spirit: it starts with hints and guiding questions rather than answers, and only walks you through a solution after you’ve shown it your real attempts. (If you ask it for the answer outright, it will politely decline — don’t take it personally.)
Two practical notes: the conversation follows you from page to page, and the Copy button in its header puts the whole transcript on your clipboard if you want to save it or bring questions to us in the fall. And the helper can make mistakes — if it ever seems confused, trust the page over the chat.

title: Reading R Code
text:
Odds and ends

Predict before you run. It’s the single highest-value habit on this site. A guess that turns out wrong teaches more than a run you didn’t think about.
If a page misbehaves — a chunk hangs, output looks stale — just reload the page. Nothing is lost but the code edits in that chunk.
Browsers: any recent Chrome, Safari, Firefox, or Edge is fine.
About AI: in the real world, AI tools will happily write R for you, and that’s fine. What they can’t do is sit in a meeting and judge whether an analysis holds up. That judgment is what you’re building here — so let the helper guide you, and keep the explaining to yourself.

See you in the fall.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
text:
Unit 1 taught you the verbs one at a time. This unit puts them together. You’ll read six pipelines that grow from three lines to a full regression, and for each one you’ll practice the core skill of this course: describing in plain English what the code did.
Work each exercise the same way:

Read the code — don’t run it yet.
Predict what the output will look like (some exercises ask you a quick question first).
Write your description in the answer box and check it.
Then run the chunk and see if the output matches what you said.

The early exercises have hints. The later ones don’t — that’s on purpose.






Note



The first chunk you run may take 20–30 seconds while R loads in your browser (this page also loads a plotting library). After that, everything is instant.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
text:
Unit 1 taught you the verbs one at a time. This unit puts them together. You’ll read six pipelines that grow from three lines to a full regression, and for each one you’ll practice the core skill of this course: describing in plain English what the code did.
Work each exercise the same way:

Read the code — don’t run it yet.
Predict what the output will look like (some exercises ask you a quick question first).
Write your description in the answer box and check it.
Then run the chunk and see if the output matches what you said.

The early exercises have hints. The later ones don’t — that’s on purpose.






Note



The first chunk you run may take 20–30 seconds while R loads in your browser (this page also loads a plotting library). After that, everything is instant.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
text:
The dataset
This unit uses a new dataset: districts, with 2022 figures for 16 Massachusetts school districts. Each row is one school district.



column
meaning




district
district name


county
county the district sits in


enrollment
number of students


pct_low_income
percent of students from low-income families


per_pupil_spend
spending per student, in dollars


math_prof
percent of students scoring proficient in math



(Figures are simplified for teaching — don’t cite them.)

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
text:
Exercise 1 — Two verbs (warm-up)








Before writing anything, make a prediction:

How many rows will the output have?

16 — filter() drops every district at or below 60% low-income; count the table above — not all of them survive.
8 — right: eight districts have pct_low_income above 60.
2 — more districts than that clear the 60% bar; check the pct_low_income column again.








TipHint





Two steps: first the rows shrink, then the columns shrink. Your description should say which rows survive (in policy terms, not code terms) and what’s left to look at.




In plain English, what does this pipeline do?

It takes the district data and keeps only the districts where more than 60% of students are from low-income families — the highest-poverty districts. Then it shows just two columns: the district name and its math proficiency rate.


A strong answer says (1) the rows are reduced to districts where more than 60 percent of students are low-income (any phrasing like “high-poverty districts,” “districts where most students are low-income” is fine as long as the direction of the condition is right), and (2) the output shows only the district name and math proficiency columns. It does not need to say how many rows result. Mark “partial” if it gets the filter direction right but forgets the column selection or vice versa. Mark “needs_work” if the filter direction is backwards (keeping low-poverty districts) or it claims columns are dropped by filter / rows by select.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
section: Exercise 2 — Creating a number that isn’t in the data
text:
Exercise 2 — Creating a number that isn’t in the data














TipHint





The middle of the pipeline creates a new column. Work out its units: students × dollars-per-student ÷ 1,000,000 = ? Then notice what arrange(desc(...)) does to the order, and which district you’d expect on top.




In plain English, what does this pipeline do, and what does the first row of the output represent?

For each district it computes total spending — enrollment times per-pupil spending — expressed in millions of dollars, then sorts the districts from biggest total budget to smallest and shows the district, its enrollment, and that total. The first row is Boston, because total spending is driven mostly by size: a big district with moderate per-pupil spending outspends a small district with high per-pupil spending.


A strong answer must (1) explain that the new column is total district spending, computed as enrollment times per-pupil spending, in millions of dollars (the millions conversion can be loosely stated), (2) note the sort is from largest to smallest total spending, and (3) identify that the top row will be a large district (naming Boston is ideal but “the biggest district” is acceptable). The key insight is that the ranking reflects district size, not generosity per student — an answer that confuses total spending with per-pupil spending is “partial” at best. “needs_work” if the student misreads mutate as filtering or doesn’t understand a new column was created.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
text:
Exercise 3 — Group and collapse









After this code runs, what does one row of the output represent?

One school district — the districts were collapsed by summarize(); they’re no longer individually visible.
One county — right: group_by(county) plus summarize() boils the data down to a single row per county.
One student — the data never had student-level rows; it started at the district level and got more aggregated, not less.



In plain English: what does this code do, and what would you tell a policymaker the avg_math_prof number for Hampden County means — including one caution about how it’s computed?

The code groups the 16 districts by county and collapses each county to one row showing the average math proficiency across its districts and the number of districts. For Hampden County, avg_math_prof is the average of the four district proficiency rates (about 25.5). The caution: it’s an unweighted average of districts — tiny Holyoke counts exactly as much as Springfield, which has almost five times the students — so it is not the proficiency rate of the average student in Hampden County.


A strong answer must (1) describe collapsing to one row per county with the mean of district proficiency and a district count, and (2) raise the unweighted-average caution: every district counts equally regardless of enrollment, so this is an average of districts, not of students. Accept any phrasing of that idea (e.g. “small districts count as much as big ones”). “partial” if the mechanics are right but the caution is missing or vague. “needs_work” if the student thinks rows are still districts or that mean() is summing.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
section: Exercise 4 — A longer pipeline, no hints
text:
Exercise 4 — A longer pipeline, no hints
This is the kind of block you’ll see in real analysis scripts: a mutate before the grouping, another after it, and a sort at the end. Take it one verb at a time.









In plain English, what does this pipeline compute, and how is the final pct_low_income different from just averaging each county’s district percentages?

First it converts each district’s low-income percentage into a count of low-income students (enrollment × percent ÷ 100). Then it groups by county and adds up, for each county, total enrollment and total low-income students. The second mutate divides those totals to get each county’s overall share of students who are low-income, and the result is sorted from highest share to lowest. This is a student-weighted figure: big districts influence it in proportion to their size. Simply averaging the district percentages would let a small district move the county number as much as a huge one — exercise 3’s caution, now fixed.


A strong answer traces all three stages: (1) converting each district’s percent to a count of low-income students, (2) summing enrollment and low-income counts within county, (3) dividing the sums to get a county-level share, sorted descending. It must also articulate the contrast: this version weights districts by enrollment (it’s the share of all students in the county), whereas averaging district percentages treats every district equally. “partial” if the mechanics are mostly right but the weighted-vs-unweighted contrast is missing or garbled. “needs_work” if the student misses that counts are computed before grouping or reads the final mutate as acting on districts rather than county totals.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
section: Exercise 5 — Reading a plot, not just a table
text:
Exercise 5 — Reading a plot, not just a table
Pipelines often end in a picture instead of a table. ggplot() gets its own treatment in the fall — for now you only need to read the aes() (“aesthetics”) line, which maps columns of the data to parts of the plot.









What does each point on this plot represent?

One school district — right: the data going in has one row per district, and nothing in the pipeline collapsed it.
One county — nothing grouped or summarized here; the counties never enter the plot.
One student — the dataset is district-level; individual students are never visible in it.


The first question to ask of any plot is the same one you ask after a summarize(): what does one mark on this chart represent? Here, district-level rows feed straight into ggplot(), so each point is a district, sized by its enrollment. The line is a simple trend line fit through the points (method = "lm" — the same lm you’ll meet in the next exercise).



Run the chunk, then describe in plain English what this figure shows. What is the relationship, and which axis is which?

Each point is a school district, plotted by its share of low-income students (horizontal axis) against its math proficiency rate (vertical axis), with bigger circles for bigger districts. The points slope strongly downward and the fitted line confirms it: districts with more low-income students have substantially lower math proficiency. The relationship is tight — most districts sit close to the line. It describes an association across districts; on its own it doesn’t say poverty causes low scores.


A strong answer identifies (1) each point as a district, (2) the axes (low-income share on x, math proficiency on y; mentioning point size as enrollment is a bonus, not required), and (3) the clear negative/downward relationship. Stating it as an association rather than causation is a plus; do not require it, but if the student makes a strong causal claim (“poverty causes low scores”), cap at “partial” and note the distinction. “needs_work” if the direction of the relationship is wrong or the axes are swapped.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
text:
Exercise 6 — A regression
The last stop in most policy analyses: a regression. lm() (“linear model”) isn’t a tidyverse verb, but you’ll see it constantly. Read the formula math_prof ~ pct_low_income + per_pupil_spend as: model math proficiency as a function of the low-income share and per-pupil spending.








Run the chunk and find the Coefficients table — it’s the part analysts actually read.

The estimate for pct_low_income is about −0.68. What does that mean here?

Comparing districts, each additional percentage point of low-income students is associated with math proficiency lower by about 0.68 points — right: a coefficient is the predicted change in the outcome per one-unit change in that variable, holding the others fixed.
68% of districts are low-income — the coefficient is a slope, not a share of districts.
Low-income students score 0.68% on the math test — the outcome is the district’s proficiency rate; the coefficient describes how it shifts, not anyone’s score.



In plain English: what question does this regression ask, and what do the results say about per-pupil spending? Mention one reason to be careful before concluding “spending doesn’t matter much.”

The regression asks how district math proficiency relates to two things at once: the share of low-income students and per-pupil spending, each holding the other constant. The low-income share dominates — strongly negative and precisely estimated. The spending coefficient looks tiny (about 0.0005) but its units are per dollar: it implies roughly half a point of proficiency per extra $1,000 per student, a modest positive association. Cautions: this is 16 districts in one year, and spending isn’t assigned randomly — high-poverty districts often receive more money precisely because they face greater needs, which can mask spending’s effect. The regression describes associations, not what would happen if a district’s budget were changed.


A strong answer must (1) describe the regression as relating math proficiency to both low-income share and spending simultaneously / holding the other constant, (2) read the spending result correctly — a small positive coefficient, ideally noticing the per-dollar units mean it’s about 0.5 points per $1,000 rather than “essentially zero”, and (3) give at least one sound caution: tiny sample, association vs. causation, or that spending may be targeted toward needier districts (reverse causality / confounding). Accept any one caution. “partial” if the regression’s purpose is right but the spending interpretation ignores units entirely or no caution is offered. “needs_work” if the student misreads which variable is the outcome or interprets coefficients as percentages of districts.

title: Unit 2 — Read a Pipeline
text:
Where you stand
If you could write a confident description for exercises 4 and 6, you have the core skill. Unit 3 raises the stakes: the pipelines will look just as tidy, but each one contains a mistake — and the output won’t warn you.

title: Unit 4 — Full Narration
text:
This is the capstone. Below is a complete analysis script of the kind a junior analyst might email around: it cleans data, builds new variables, summarizes, plots, and runs a regression — about 35 lines, start to finish. Your job is the one you’ll actually face this fall: read someone else’s analysis and narrate what they did, then flag what you’d push back on.
There are no hints on this page. There are a few quick checkpoint questions to keep you oriented, and two written responses at the end — the narration itself, and the decision you’d question.

title: Unit 4 — Full Narration
text:
This is the capstone. Below is a complete analysis script of the kind a junior analyst might email around: it cleans data, builds new variables, summarizes, plots, and runs a regression — about 35 lines, start to finish. Your job is the one you’ll actually face this fall: read someone else’s analysis and narrate what they did, then flag what you’d push back on.
There are no hints on this page. There are a few quick checkpoint questions to keep you oriented, and two written responses at the end — the narration itself, and the decision you’d question.

title: Unit 4 — Full Narration
text:
The data
The script analyzes insurance_panel: uninsured rates for the same ten states you know from Units 1 and 3, but now tracked over time — one row per state per year, 2014–2022 (90 rows). It’s pre-loaded; run the chunk to see its shape.







column
meaning




state, year
which state, which year


population_m
state population, millions


expanded
TRUE if the state expanded Medicaid


uninsured_rate
share of residents uninsured (proportion, 0–1)


unemployment_rate
share of labor force unemployed (proportion, 0–1)



(Synthetic data, patterned on real trends — don’t cite it.)






















TipTwo functions you haven’t seen



The script uses if_else(condition, a, b) (pick a where the condition holds, b elsewhere) and weighted.mean(x, w) (a mean where each value counts in proportion to its weight w). Part of reading real code is meeting unfamiliar functions and inferring what they do from their names and inputs — these two work exactly the way they sound.

title: Unit 4 — Full Narration
text:
The script
Read the whole thing before running anything. The chunk produces three outputs: a summary table, a figure, and a regression table.

title: Unit 4 — Full Narration
text:
Checkpoints
Quick orientation checks before you write. Run the script first if you haven’t.

1. Which step changes the number of rows in analysis compared to insurance_panel?

The filter(!is.na(uninsured_rate)) line, which drops the two state-years with missing rates — right: 90 rows in, 88 out. Nothing else adds or removes rows.
The mutate() block — mutate() adds columns; the row count is untouched.
The group_by() |> summarize() step — that collapses rows, but in trend, a separate object; analysis itself keeps all 88 rows.



2. In the trend table, what does one row represent?

One state in one year — that was analysis; the summarize() collapsed the states.
One expansion group in one year — right: group_by(group, year) leaves one row per group per year, 18 rows in all.
One state across all years — the years are still separate; it’s the states that were combined.



3. The regression coefficient on expandedTRUE is about −10. Why would it be a mistake to read that as “expansion reduced uninsurance by 10 points”?

Because the coefficient isn’t statistically significant — it’s highly significant; significance isn’t the issue here.
Because it compares expansion states to non-expansion states in levels, and those states differed long before expansion — Texas and Florida had far higher uninsurance than Massachusetts back in 2014 — right: the regression mostly captures pre-existing differences between the two groups, not the change expansion caused.
Because the unemployment control ruined the model — its coefficient is near zero; including it barely changes anything.


Look back at the trend table: the gap between the groups was already about 8 points in 2014. The regression pools all years and compares group levels, so most of that −10 is history, not policy effect. The plot actually contains the better evidence: the expansion group’s line falls faster (roughly −4 points vs. −2 points over the period). Comparing changes rather than levels is the heart of the difference-in-differences designs you’ll meet this fall.

title: Unit 4 — Full Narration
text:
Your narration

Write 2–3 paragraphs narrating this analysis for a colleague who hasn’t seen the code: what data went in, what the analyst did to it (cleaning, transformations, summaries), what each output shows, and what the headline finding is. Use plain English — no function names required.

The analyst starts with a state-year panel: uninsured and unemployment rates for ten states, each year from 2014 to 2022, plus population and whether the state expanded Medicaid. Two state-year observations are missing an uninsured rate and get dropped, leaving 88 rows. The rates are rescaled from proportions to percentages, and each state is labeled by expansion status.
Next the analyst collapses the data to compare the two groups over time: for each group in each year, a population-weighted average uninsured rate, so big states like California and Texas count in proportion to their size. The printed table snapshots 2014, 2018, and 2022; the line chart shows the full series. Both tell the same story: expansion states started around 12% uninsured and fell to about 8%, while non-expansion states started much higher, around 20%, and drifted down only a couple of points.
Finally, a regression relates the uninsured percentage to expansion status and the unemployment rate. Expansion states average about 10 percentage points lower uninsurance, a large and statistically strong gap; unemployment shows essentially no association in this sample. The headline: expansion states have far less uninsurance and saw faster declines. But the regression compares levels across states that differed before expansion, so the −10 shouldn’t be read as the causal effect of expanding.


Grade as a narration, not a quiz. A strong answer covers, in coherent prose: (1) the data — state-year panel, ten states, 2014-2022, with expansion status; (2) cleaning — dropping the two missing-rate observations (mentioning ~88 remaining rows is bonus) and converting proportions to percentages; (3) the group-year summary — average uninsured rate by expansion group over time, and ideally that it is population-weighted; (4) what the plot/table show — expansion group lower and falling faster (any reasonable reading of levels and trends is fine); (5) the regression — expansion associated with roughly 10 points lower uninsurance, unemployment near zero. Do not require exact numbers; the shape of the story matters more. “strong” requires at least four of the five elements woven into readable prose. “partial” for answers that hit roughly three, or that list steps mechanically without saying what the outputs mean. “needs_work” if major steps are misread (e.g., thinks the regression is at the person level, misses the cleaning entirely, or reverses which group has lower uninsurance).



Name ONE decision this analyst made that you would question before circulating the results, and say why it matters. (There are several defensible choices — pick the one you’d raise first.)

The one I’d raise first: presenting the regression coefficient (−10) as the expansion result. It compares average levels between expansion and non-expansion states, but those groups were far apart before expansion did anything — the 2014 gap is already about 8 points. A levels comparison mostly repackages pre-existing differences; comparing each group’s change over time (about −4 vs. −2 points) is the more honest summary, and even that rests on only three non-expansion states. Other defensible answers: dropping the two missing state-years without asking why they’re missing; treating expansion as a fixed yes/no when states expanded in different years; the near-meaningless unemployment control; or generalizing from ten hand-picked states.


Accept ANY ONE well-argued concern; do not require the model answer’s choice. Defensible targets include: levels-vs-changes / pre-existing differences in the regression (the strongest catch); silently dropping the two missing observations; coding expansion as time-invariant when timing varies; only three non-expansion states / tiny sample; no causal identification; generalizing from ten states; weighting choices. “strong” requires naming a specific decision in the script AND a clear account of why it could change the conclusion — not just “correlation isn’t causation” boilerplate. “partial” for a valid concern stated vaguely or without consequences. “needs_work” for concerns about things the script didn’t do, or pure style complaints (variable names, plot colors).

title: Unit 4 — Full Narration
text:
What’s next
That’s the primer. If you can narrate this script and spot the soft spot in its regression, you’re ready for the fall: we’ll start from exactly this skill and add the tools — better designs, real data, and the statistics to back up the instincts you’ve been building.
